petitioner_background: The petitioner is a journalist, currently a contributing writer for an unnamed publication and a writer and U.S. correspondent for an independent Russian radio station. Previously, she worked as a radio host in Russia, as a television host for an independent television channel in Moscow, and as a writer for a Ukrainian daily newspaper. She focuses on Russian-American politics, U.S. politics, foreign policy, and foreign relations. She sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under INA § 203(b)(1)(A) (EB-1A).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted online articles from The Guardian and Politico in which she discusses her work as a journalist in Russia.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director found, and the AAO agreed, that the petitioner met this criterion. The Guardian and Politico published online articles discussing the petitioner's work as a journalist in Russia, satisfying the requirement for published material about her in professional or major trade publications or other major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence related to original contributions of major significance, though the specific evidence is not detailed in the decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined that the petitioner submitted evidence related to this criterion but did not satisfy it. The petitioner did not contest this issue on appeal, and the AAO deemed the issue waived per Matter of M-A-S-, 24 I&N Dec. 762, 767 n.2 (BIA 2009).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a co-authored article that was cited in a New York Times bestselling book; (2) articles published on the website of a quarterly magazine described by its publisher as 'a premier platform for political thought...read in the corridors of government and academia...and also read by a more general public of subscribers and visitors to our website'; (3) a letter from the publisher of the magazine; (4) additional letters from staff of the publication; (5) profiles of the editorial board's members; (6) evidence that another of her articles was referenced in a book; and (7) testimonials from academics and journalists in the field of Russian-American politics who stated they read and value her articles. She did not submit evidence that she had been published in the print version of the magazine, only on its website.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that the petitioner did not establish the scholarly nature of her published articles. Outside the academic arena, a scholarly article must be written for 'learned' persons in the field, defined as having or demonstrating profound knowledge or scholarship. While the magazine and many of the petitioner's articles may appeal to the interests of academia and government, the publisher confirmed its articles are intended to be accessible to the general public, particularly the online articles. The intended audience of the petitioner's articles was not established to be 'learned persons' in the field. Initially, the petitioner claimed her article qualified as scholarly only because it was cited in a New York Times bestseller, but did not explain how that citation met the scholarly threshold or that the book itself was a professional/major trade publication. Additionally, the petitioner did not address the second element of the criterion — she did not submit evidence establishing that the magazine is a professional or trade publication, or that it has the circulation to be considered a major medium. The Director also found her published pieces were 'journalistic articles' with an intended audience of 'the general public.'

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed critical roles with a Russian radio station and another organization. She submitted letters from the editor-in-chief of the radio station containing detailed and probative information regarding her role as a co-host of multiple prime time radio programs, as the radio station's only U.S. correspondent, and as the most-read contributor to the radio station's website. She also submitted evidence of the distinguished reputation of both organizations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director found sufficient evidence of the distinguished reputation of both organizations but determined the testimonial letters did not indicate her duties were leading for the organization as a whole or that her accomplishments went beyond providing contractual services. The AAO disagreed with the Director and found that the petitioner submitted sufficient evidence to establish she performed in a critical role with the Russian radio station. The editor-in-chief's letters contained detailed and probative information regarding her role as a co-host of multiple prime time radio programs, as the station's only U.S. correspondent, and as the most-read contributor to the website. The AAO held that it is not the title of the role but rather the performance in the role that determines whether the role is critical, and the petitioner met this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The appeal was dismissed because the petitioner met only two of the ten initial evidentiary criteria (published material and leading/critical role), falling short of the required three. The AAO rejected the petitioner's request to consider comparable evidence, holding that the regulations do not allow substitution of evidence based on a claim it is comparable to criteria from a separate nonimmigrant classification (O-1), and that the petitioner did not adequately show why the EB-1A criteria do not apply to her occupation as a journalist — her general assertions were not probative and she initially claimed to meet four criteria. Because the initial evidence threshold was not met, no final merits determination under Kazarian was required, though the AAO noted it reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding of sustained national or international acclaim or that the petitioner is among the small percentage at the very top of her field. The AAO emphasized the EB-1A is a highly restrictive classification for those already at the top of their fields, not those progressing toward the top.
legal_citations: Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)

USCIS Policy Memorandum PM-602-0005.1, Evaluation of Evidence Submitted with Certain Form I-140 Petitions; Revisions to the Adjudicator's Field Manual (AFM) Chapter 22.2, AFM Update AD11-14 (Dec. 22, 2010)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner waived the original contributions criterion by not contesting it on appeal, per Matter of M-A-S-. The petitioner also improperly attempted to invoke comparable evidence by referencing a criterion from the O-1 nonimmigrant classification regulations rather than explaining why the EB-1A criteria do not apply to her occupation.
